xi_cao.name: Xi Cao
xi_cao.summary:
Representing Maryland, Xi Cao has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Xi's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

What makes Xi especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (7 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 20 different doubles partners over the years, Xi adapts to anyone:
  • Katherine F Xu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Adithya S Burisetty (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Xi is peaking at the right time in 2025-2026, with 1 title across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Xi is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Xi has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Xi isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 11 seeded entries across the career, Xi consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 1 title, Xi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
xi_cao.roast:
The court is set, the shuttle is ready, and here comes Xi Cao, from Maryland, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Xi has 49 career entries across 21 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Xi saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 Capital NE OLC in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Xi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Boys' Singles finals and Xi — name a more iconic tragedy. 2 trips to the championship match, and Xi has turned 2 of them into silver medals. At this point the engraver probably just pre-stamps '2nd' on Xi's trophies to save time.

We need to address the elephant in the room — no, not the travel costs, the OTHER elephant: what happens when Xi reaches a final. Xi has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Xi does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Xi has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Xi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Xi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Xi is 0-for-21. Zero titles in 21 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Xi and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Xi needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Xi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Xi's texts. The go-to partner is Jonathan Wu (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in BD U13 — we hope Jason Gu has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Xi's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Xi has shown up 7 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7. Tournament committees have handed Xi a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Xi has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) in BS U15, where Xi was seeded #2 and finished 9th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Xi has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Xi has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Xi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U15, which means Xi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Xi entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Xi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Xi story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 1 title across 5 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who the undisputed champion of second place in Boys' Singles. But every season Xi comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Xi, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

What makes Xiaoan especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Xiaoan has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

xiaoan_fan.roast:
Allow us to introduce Xiaoan Fan, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 13 events across 6 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 13-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Xiaoan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Xiaoan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Xiaoan saga began at 2024 South CRC (TBTT) with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Xiaoan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 13 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Xiaoan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Derik S Li has emotionally recovered.

Xiaoan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Xiaoan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Xiaoan has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Xiaoan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13, which means Xiaoan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Xiaoan entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Xiaoan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Xiaoan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Xiaoan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Xiaoan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence.

xiaoyue_liu.name: Xiaoyue Liu
xiaoyue_liu.summary:
Hailing from California, Xiaoyue Liu has reached the podium at Junior Nationals, finishing top-4 in GD U19. At the biggest event on the calendar, making the final four is a statement: Xiaoyue belongs with the best in the country.

What makes Xiaoyue especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #3, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Xiaoyue is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
xiaoyue_liu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Xiaoyue Liu, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-4. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Xiaoyue has 53 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 2% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Xiaoyue saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Dave Freeman Jr OLC in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Xiaoyue's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 1 title in 22 entries — a stretch where Xiaoyue actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Xiaoyue is 0-for-8. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

The seeding trajectory in GS U19 tells a story: #1 at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC, down to #2 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. When the people whose job is to rank you start ranking you lower, that is not a slump — that is a trend line with a very clear direction, and it is not pointing up.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Xiaoyue would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 9 times Xiaoyue has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 9 times Xiaoyue has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Xiaoyue's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Xiaoyue plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Xiaoyue is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Xiaoyue has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Xiaoyue's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Chloe Lim: 10 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 10 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Chloe Lim has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15 — we hope Stanley Xing has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Xiaoyue's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. Junior Nationals has seen Xiaoyue 6 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Then there is the seeding situation. Xiaoyue has been seeded in 8 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Xiaoyue takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U19, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Xiaoyue. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Xiaoyue is not just losing locally — Xiaoyue is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Liu family travels cross-country so Xiaoyue can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Xiaoyue has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Xiaoyue showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in XD U15, which means Xiaoyue traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Xiaoyue entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Xiaoyue paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

In the end, Xiaoyue's record reads like a choose-your-own-adventure book where most paths lead to the parking lot. 1 title shine bright, but they are surrounded by 52 entries that ended with a handshake and a quiet walk off court. For context: Xiaoyue is a player who treats seedings like suggestions rather than expectations. Will Xiaoyue figure it out? Will the next tournament be the breakthrough? History says no. The human spirit says maybe. Xiaoyue's credit card statement says the entry fee is already paid. See you at the next one.

xiner_hu.roast:
Clear your schedule, because we need to talk about Xiner Hu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has stood at the top of the podium at Junior Nationals — the crown jewel of the American junior badminton calendar — and won GD U11. That is a real, verified, indisputable accomplishment, and no one can ever take it away. But here's what they CAN take away: any illusion that the rest of this resume is equally impressive. Because we are looking at 33 career entries across 11 tournaments, 8 total wins, and 25 losses. A 24% win rate that suggests the Nationals title was less a coronation and more a cosmic accident that Xiner has been unsuccessfully trying to replicate ever since. The story started at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, and it has been a wild, inconsistent, deeply roastable ride from there.

The Xiner saga began at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Xiner's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 5 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Xiner actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 3 wins in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Xiner plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 2 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Xiner can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Xiner has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Xiner's texts. The most successful partnership is with Naksh Shetty — 8 events, 3 titles — and honestly, Naksh Shetty deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Naksh Shetty a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11 — we hope Jeremy Wang has emotionally recovered.

Tournament committees have handed Xiner a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Xiner has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships in GS U13, where Xiner was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

One thing nobody can accuse Xiner of is playing it safe. Xiner has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Xiner has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Xiner has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Xiner is not just losing locally — Xiner is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 24% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Hu family travels cross-country so Xiner can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Xiner has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Xiner showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U11, which means Xiner traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Xiner entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 18th, which is a polite way of saying Xiner paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The Xiner story is not a tragedy — it is a very long comedy with occasional dramatic moments. 8 titles across 2 seasons is not nothing — it is just a lot less than the talent and the frequent-flyer miles would suggest. After all, this is someone who changes doubles partners more often than most people change phone cases. But every season Xiner comes back, and every season we get new material. For that alone, Xiner, we are genuinely grateful. Never change. Or actually, please change — the finals record is giving us anxiety.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Xinyang saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 Dave Freeman OLC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Xinyang's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 2 titles in 12 entries — a stretch where Xinyang actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 11 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

If Xinyang's career were a movie, the finals record would be the part where the audience starts stress-eating popcorn. Here goes. Xinyang has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2022 SoCal CRC, where Xinyang entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U17, BD U17). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Xinyang does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Xinyang has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 3 wins means Xinyang is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Xinyang plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Xinyang is 0-for-14. Zero titles in 14 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Xinyang and suddenly the wins start appearing — 3 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Xinyang needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Xinyang has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Xinyang's texts. The go-to partner is Cassidy Yeh (6 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17 — we hope Hansika - Malladi has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Xinyang's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Xinyang has shown up 6 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Xinyang has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Xinyang is not just losing locally — Xinyang is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Peng family travels cross-country so Xinyang can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Xinyang has 16 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Xinyang showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U15, which means Xinyang traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC, where Xinyang entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 26th, which is a polite way of saying Xinyang paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 3 titles, 41 entries, and 38 that ended without a trophy. A player who treats championship matches like optional practice sessions. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the moments of brilliance — Xinyang looks like a contender. We did not squint. We used a microscope. And the data paints a portrait of a player who is equal parts talented and maddening — the kind who gives you just enough to keep believing, and then just enough disappointment to keep you humble. The shuttle tube industry thanks you, Xinyang. We will be watching.
